explicit_text: I was born in the Philippines, specifically in Quezon City, Manila, in 1962. Growing up there, I experienced island life and a tropical climate before moving to the United States in 1975. My Filipino identity has always been a core part of who I am, especially as I navigated life as an immigrant.

explicit_text: I moved to the United States in 1975 when I was thirteen, hoping for better opportunities. My early years here were challenging due to racial discrimination, but I eventually found a supportive Filipino American community. Becoming a U.S. citizen at eighteen helped me align with American norms while still embracing my Filipino heritage.

explicit_text: The 1965 Immigration Act was crucial for my family’s immigration journey because it promoted family reunification. Thanks to this law, my mother and I were able to join relatives already living in the United States. It opened the door for us to come to America in 1975 and seek new opportunities.

explicit_text: When my mother and I first arrived in the United States in April 1975, we landed in Honolulu, Hawaii. Honolulu was a common entry point for many Filipino immigrants after the 1965 Immigration Act. It was the first step in our new life before moving to the mainland U.S.

explicit_text: Tagalog was my first language, and when I started school in the U.S., I only spoke minimal English. I was often teased for my Filipino accent and the way I spoke Tagalog. Later, when I moved to Los Angeles, I found a community where I could speak Tagalog freely and reconnect with my culture.

explicit_text: In July 1977, my mother and I moved to Rampart, a neighborhood in Los Angeles also known as Historic Filipinotown. Although Rampart was known for crime, it became a place where we reconnected with our Filipino identity. Living among Filipino families, I attended cultural gatherings and felt a renewed sense of belonging.

explicit_text: I attended Immaculate Heart High School in Los Angeles starting at age sixteen. It was an all-girls Catholic school with many Filipina and Latina students, which made me feel comfortable speaking Tagalog and expressing my culture. This school was a safe space where I could be myself without fear of judgment.

explicit_text: Today, I live in Agoura Hills, California, which is not far from my old neighborhood in Rampart, Los Angeles. Living here allows me to stay connected with the Filipino community and share our culture and pride. It’s a place where I find joy and a sense of belonging after all the challenges I faced.
